key_points: Functional groups are atoms or groups of atoms that join a carbon chain and determine the chemical properties of the compound.
A Homologous Series is a group of organic compounds having the same general formula and functional group, where successive members differ by a $-CH_2$ unit.
Isomers are compounds that have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements (e.g., Butane and Iso-butane).
IUPAC nomenclature is the systematic method used for naming organic chemical compounds.
The physical properties of a homologous series change regularly (e.g., boiling point increases) as the molecular mass increases.
examples: Functional Groups: Alcohol ($-OH$), Aldehyde ($-CHO$), Ketone ($>C=O$), and Carboxylic Acid ($-COOH$).
Isomers of Pentane: $n$-pentane, Isopentane, and Neopentane (all $C_5H_{12}$).
Homologous Difference: The molecular mass difference between any two adjacent members of a series is 14 u.
Structural formula: Propanone ($CH_3-CO-CH_3$), the simplest ketone.
formulas: Carboxylic acid suffix: -oic acid (Example: Ethanoic acid, CH₃COOH)
exam_tips: When naming, always identify the longest carbon chain first to determine the root word (Meth-, Eth-, Prop-, etc.).
Remember that Ketones can never be at the end of a carbon chain; they must be between two carbon atoms.
Note that the chemical properties of members in a homologous series remain similar because they share the same functional group.
In isomers, as branching increases, the surface area decreases, usually leading to a lower boiling point.
